message:
perf(core): eliminate redundant background-status checks and lazify tracing span creation

Connection paths like query(), execute(), prepare(), and prepared
statement step/query now hoist background_status() to the outermost
entry point and thread an *_after_background_status suffix through the
internal dispatch chain. This avoids the common pattern where a single
user-facing call would check background_status() 2-3 times (once at
the public API boundary, once in execute_statement(), and once in
op_cx()).

Three new hot-path profile counters quantify the improvement:
- FSQLITE_BACKGROUND_STATUS_CHECKS: total mutex acquisitions
- FSQLITE_OP_CX_BACKGROUND_GATES: op_cx() calls that still re-check
- FSQLITE_STATEMENT_DISPATCH_BACKGROUND_GATES: legacy dispatch path

Tracing spans throughout the prepare/execute pipeline are now created
lazily behind tracing::enabled!() guards. Previously, every operation
unconditionally allocated spans for fsqlite.parse, fsqlite.statement,
fsqlite.plan, fsqlite.compat_trace, and fsqlite::cx targets even when
no subscriber was collecting at that level. The lazy pattern uses
.then(|| { span }) with .as_ref().map(Span::enter) to preserve the
guard lifetime while skipping allocation entirely when tracing is off.

RuntimeContext now captures the ambient asupersync RuntimeHandle at
construction time (native feature only) and stores it as an optional
field. The write coordinator service startup path uses this stored
handle instead of calling Runtime::current_handle() at launch time,
ensuring the coordinator runs on the runtime that was active when the
connection environment was created, not whatever runtime happens to be
ambient when the service starts. A new integration test validates that
dropping the ambient runtime does not kill the coordinator when it was
launched from a different env-specified runtime.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
